text: Robotic single incision inguinal hernia repair by Dr. Clark Gerhart.
Robotic single incision inguinal hernia repair by Dr. Clark Gerhart.
The surgeon initiates the robotic single incision inguinal hernia repair by creating three small stab wounds at the umbilicus: one at the superior rim, one at the base, and one at the inferior rim.
The surgeon initiates the operation by creating three small stab wounds at the umbilicus: one on the superior rim, one at the base, and one on the inferior rim.
The surgeon places trocars through the three stab wounds at the umbilicus, preparing for robotic single incision inguinal hernia repair without additional incisions.
Three small stab wounds at the umbilicus are used for the placement of trocars at a single site, with no additional incisions visible.
The surgeon enters the abdomen bluntly with direct visualization using a laparoscope, observing the layers of the abdominal wall as they are traversed.
The surgeon enters the abdomen bluntly, using direct visualization through the laparoscope to observe the layers of the abdominal wall as they are traversed.
The surgeon safely enters the abdomen and begins insufflation, expanding the abdominal cavity for better visualization and access during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon safely enters the abdomen and begins insufflation, expanding the abdominal cavity for better visualization and access.
The surgeon inspects the abdominal cavity for any additional pathology or bilateral hernias using the laparoscopic view.
The surgeon inspects the abdominal cavity for any additional pathology or the presence of bilateral hernias.

A trocar is placed in the superior rim of the umbilicus, followed by the insertion of robotic arms number one and two at the base and inferior rim. The robotic arms are positioned to cross at the level of the fascia.

A trocar is placed in the superior rim of the umbilicus, followed by the placement of robotic arms number one and two at the base and inferior rim. These robotic arms are positioned to cross at the level of the fascia.
The surgeon positions the remote centers of the robotic arms at the level of the fascia, ensuring optimal flexibility for the three arms during the hernia repair procedure.
The remote centers of the robotic arms are positioned at the level of the fascia, ensuring optimal flexibility for the surgical procedure.
The surgeon places the camera port into the abdominal wall, which includes an insufflation port for CO₂ insufflation, allowing for visualization of the surgical field.
The surgeon places the camera port into the abdomen, which includes an insufflation port for CO₂ insufflation.
The surgeon replaces the initial five millimeter port with robotic ports, inserting them through the lower two stab wounds in the abdominal wall.
The surgeon replaces the initial five millimeter port with robotic ports, inserting them through the lower two stab wounds in the abdominal wall.
The surgeon demonstrates the crossed port sites used for the robotic arms, positioned at the level of the fascia, to perform the hernia repair operation.
The surgeon demonstrates the placement of port sites, which are crossed at the level of the fascia, to facilitate the robotic operation.
The reason that these work as crossed instruments, even though they're straight, is the fact that the robotic instruments have long wrists, especially the five millimeter instruments shown here.
The robotic instruments, particularly the five millimeter ones, are shown with long wrists that enable them to function effectively as crossed instruments despite being straight.
The robotic arms are positioned to work independently, utilizing long-jawed instruments to achieve further separation and flexibility during the hernia repair procedure.
The robotic arms are positioned to work independently, utilizing long-jawed instruments to achieve further separation and flexibility during the procedure.

The surgeon attaches the robotic arms to the ports, starting with the camera arm, followed by arm number two on the left side connected to the five millimeter port, and then arm number one. The robotic arms visibly cross at the level of the fascia.

The surgeon attaches the robotic arms to the ports, starting with the camera arm. Arm number two is attached to the five millimeter port on the left side, followed by arm number one. The robotic arms cross at the level of the fascia.

The camera is positioned at the superior rim of the umbilicus using a thirty-degree up viewing scope, providing a clear view of the instruments positioned anterior to the camera and a direct view of the inguinal region where the dissection will occur.

The camera is positioned in the superior rim of the umbilicus using a thirty-degree up viewing scope, providing a clear view of the instruments positioned anterior to the camera and a plain view of the inguinal region where the dissection will occur.
The operation itself then looks very much like a standard tap transabdominal preperitoneal operation.
The operation itself then looks very much like a standard tap transabdominal preperitoneal operation.
The surgeon begins the procedure by incising the peritoneum anterior to the inguinal defect, then strips the peritoneum from the underside of the groin, exposing the surgical area for hernia repair.
The surgeon begins the procedure by incising the peritoneum anterior to the inguinal defect and stripping the peritoneum from the underside of the groin.

The articulating wristed robotic instruments are visible, demonstrating adequate separation to prevent clashing. The camera is actively repositioned to maintain proper orientation and minimize conflict between the three robotic arms during the hernia repair procedure.
The articulating wristed robotic instruments are visible, allowing for adequate separation of the robotic arms to prevent clashing during the procedure.
The surgeon adjusts the camera orientation to maintain alignment with the robotic arms, ensuring minimal conflict between the three arms during the operation.
The surgeon dissects the peritoneal hernia sac from the spermatic cord, primarily using blunt dissection techniques, with occasional sharp or cautery dissection as required.
The surgeon dissects the peritoneal hernia sac off the spermatic cord, primarily using blunt dissection, with occasional sharp or cautery dissection as required.
The edge of the hernia sac is clearly visible, enhanced by the robotic visualization, providing a detailed view of the anatomical structure during the hernia repair procedure.
The edge of the hernia sac is clearly visible, enhanced by the robotic visualization, highlighting the anatomical structure during the procedure.
The surgeon utilizes a hook cautery from the five millimeter robotic set to achieve hemostasis, addressing any bleeding observed in the surgical field.
The surgeon utilizes a hook cautery from the five millimeter robotic set to achieve hemostasis as needed during the procedure.
The surgeon continues dissecting the hernia sac from the spermatic cord, ensuring it is elevated sufficiently to prevent mesh curling during placement, which could lead to recurrence.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the hernia sac along the spermatic cord, ensuring it is dissected sufficiently to prevent mesh curling and potential recurrence.
The surgeon clears the surgical area, creating adequate space for the placement of the mesh.
The surgeon clears the surgical area, creating adequate space for the placement of the mesh.
The surgeon inserts the mesh through the larger camera port, measuring eight and a half millimeters, into the surgical field for hernia repair.
The surgeon inserts the mesh through the larger camera port, measuring eight and a half millimeters, into the surgical field. The mesh is positioned for hernia repair using robotic assistance.

The surgeon uses Parietex ProGrip mesh, characterized by its Velcro-like backing, to secure it to the underlying structures. Needle holders are employed as graspers, providing high grip strength for precise handling of the mesh.

The surgeon uses Parietex ProGrip mesh, characterized by its Velcro-like backing, to secure it to underlying structures. Needle holders are employed as graspers, providing high grip strength for precise handling of the mesh.

The surgeon uses high grip strength needle holders to strip the peritoneal hernia sac from the cord structures, ensuring secure handling of the peritoneum. The mesh is positioned and can be sutured in place if necessary.
The surgeon uses high grip strength instruments to securely grasp the peritoneum while stripping the peritoneal hernia sac during the dissection of the cord structures.
The surgeon prepares to suture the mesh in place within the surgical field, ensuring it is securely positioned to prevent movement.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum using a V-lock absorbable suture, efficiently anchoring the suture with its looped back end, eliminating the need for knot tying.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum using a V-lock absorbable suture, efficiently securing the tissue with the suture's looped back end.
The surgeon uses wristed instruments to run the V-lock absorbable suture, creating an anchor without knot tying. The suture efficiently closes the peritoneum securely, preventing gapping.
The surgeon uses wristed instruments to run the suture, securely closing the peritoneum without any gapping, ensuring a tight closure in this portion of the hernia repair procedure.
A potential advantage of the robotic technique is to avoid tacks, which can cause chronic pain, and they are also expensive. reduction in cost is also evident.

The surgeon completes the suture line by backing up the V-lock suture on itself with several throws, securing the closure without the need for knot tying.
The surgeon completes the suture line by backing up the V-lock suture on itself with a few throws, finalizing the closure without the need for a knot.
The assistant uses robotic scissors to cut the suture, entering through the second robotic port and positioning in front of the camera for visibility.
The assistant uses robotic scissors to cut the suture, entering through the second robotic port and positioning in front of the camera.
